architecture.summary: ThinkPRM is a generative process reward model that verifies solution steps by generating extended verification chains-of-thought (CoTs). It fine-tunes open-weight reasoning models (R1-Distill-Qwen and QwQ) on synthetic verification CoTs collected via rejection sampling from QwQ-32B-Preview, filtered to match gold step-level labels from PRM800K. The model generates step-by-step verification rationales with explicit correctness judgments for each step, enabling both parallel scaling (multiple independent CoTs, averaged) and sequential scaling (self-correction/double-checking via trigger phrases). Information flows from problem-solution pairs through the reasoning model's CoT generation, with step correctness extracted from marked tokens (\boxed{yes}/\boxed{no}).

baseline.name: DiscPRM trained on PRM800K (712K process labels)

claims.statement: ThinkPRM-14B, trained on 8K process labels or 1K synthetic examples, outperforms discriminative PRMs trained on about 100x more data
ThinkPRM-1.5B, trained using the same 8K labels, outperforms LLM-as-a-judge and discriminative verifiers in reward-guided search on MATH-500
On ProcessBench, ThinkPRM substantially reduces invalid judgments (verification CoTs without extractable label) compared to LLM-as-a-Judge
On GPQA-Diamond physics subset, ThinkPRM surpasses discriminative verifiers trained on full PRM800K by 8%
On LiveCodeBench (200-problem subset), ThinkPRM surpasses discriminative verifiers trained on full PRM800K by 4.5%
Under same token budget, ThinkPRM scales up verification compute more effectively compared to LLM-as-a-Judge, outperforming it by 7.2% on a subset of ProcessBench
Training on long synthetic verification CoTs substantially outperforms training on short, compressed versions of the same CoTs
Fine-tuning on synthetic verification CoTs substantially reduces overthinking and infinite looping behavior in reasoning models used as verifiers
